confidence.disclosure: Al-Shafi'i is one of the best-mapped early Muslim scholars: Gaza birth in 767, his mother's move to Mecca around age two, study with Malik in Medina until Malik's death in 795, the Yemen governorship at age thirty (around 797), the arrest in 803 and the journey in chains to Caliph Harun al-Rashid at Raqqa, the years in Baghdad with al-Shaybani, return to Mecca in 804, second Baghdad period from 810, departure for Egypt in 814, and death in Fustat on 19 January 820. The biographical sources (Ibn Abi Hatim, Bayhaqi, Ibn Khallikan) all agree on the broad chronology and many specific dates. Day-precision is available for his death.

sources: Ibn Abi Hatim al-Razi, Adab al-Shafi'i wa Manaqibuhu
Joseph Schacht, The Origins of Muhammadan Jurisprudence (1950)
Kecia Ali, Imam Shafi'i: Scholar and Saint (2011)

journey.event: Born in Gaza in Palestine in 150 AH (767 CE), the same year that Imam Abu Hanifa dies in Baghdad — a coincidence later read as a passing of the torch in jurisprudence. His father is a soldier from Mecca stationed in the Gaza garrison; his mother is from a Yemeni family of the Azd tribe.

His father dies in Gaza when Shafi'i is still an infant. Fearing the loss of his noble Qurayshi lineage, his mother takes him to Mecca, where the family of his father has roots. He grows up in poverty in the holy city.

Memorises the entire Qur'an by age seven. According to tradition, his mother cannot afford paper, so he writes his lessons on shoulder bones and shards. Studies under Muslim ibn Khalid al-Zanji, the leading mufti of Mecca.
By the age of ten he has memorised the Muwatta — Imam Malik's foundational legal collection — from a borrowed copy. Recites it in the streets of Mecca to the astonishment of his neighbours.
At fifteen, the chief judge of Mecca permits him to issue legal rulings (fatwas) — a remarkable young licence indicating both his command of the law and his maturity.

Travels to Medina to study with Imam Malik ibn Anas himself. Reportedly recites the entire Muwatta from memory at his first meeting with Malik, who is so impressed he says: 'God has placed a light in your heart, young man — do not extinguish it with disobedience.' Stays in Medina for nearly a decade.
Imam Malik dies in Medina. By this time Shafi'i has gained a reputation as one of the most brilliant young jurists of the age. He always refers to Malik afterwards as 'al-Ustadh' (the Teacher).

Travels to Yemen, where his mother's family lives, in search of employment. The Abbasid governor appoints him as a judge in Najran. He is famous for incorruptibility — refusing the bribes that local notables expect to use to influence verdicts. This makes him many enemies.

Falsely accused by his enemies in Yemen of plotting on behalf of the Alid Shi'a. Arrested in chains and sent across the desert to Caliph Harun al-Rashid, who is then at his northern capital of Raqqa on the Euphrates. Several of his fellow accused are executed at the caliph's order; Shafi'i is brought before the caliph last and defends himself with such eloquence that Harun acquits him on the spot.

After his acquittal, the caliph's chief judge Muhammad ibn al-Hasan al-Shaybani — the leading living disciple of Abu Hanifa — invites Shafi'i to Baghdad. He moves into Shaybani's house and studies the Hanafi legal system at its source, debating Shaybani for two years on the differences between the schools.

Returns to Mecca and begins teaching at the Sacred Mosque. His circle attracts students from across the Muslim world, including the young Ahmad ibn Hanbal, who is so impressed that he later says: 'Whenever knowledge is mentioned, Shafi'i is a star.'

Returns to Baghdad in 810. By now his stature is such that he establishes an independent school of legal thought there — what later jurists call al-madhhab al-qadim, his 'old school'. Caliph al-Ma'mun reportedly offers him a judgeship, which he refuses.

Disgusted by the Abbasid civil war then raging between al-Amin and al-Ma'mun, leaves Baghdad for Egypt. In Fustat (old Cairo) he begins teaching at the mosque of Amr ibn al-As. The change of environment leads him to substantially revise his legal methodology — what scholars now call his 'new school' (al-madhhab al-jadid).

In Egypt, dictates al-Risala — the first systematic work on usul al-fiqh, the principles of Islamic jurisprudence. The book defines for the first time the four sources of law (Qur'an, Sunna, ijma, qiyas) and shapes every legal school after him.
His health begins to fail under the pressure of constant teaching and writing. He continues to lead study circles even when bedridden.

Dies on the night of 19 January 820 (last day of Rajab 204 AH) in Fustat, Egypt, aged 53. Asked on his deathbed why he carried a walking stick when he was not lame, he had once answered: 'To remind myself that I am on a journey out of this life.' Buried at the foot of the Muqattam hills; the Mamluk-era shrine over his tomb is still one of the largest in Egypt.
